examples.title: Internal vs External Commands Demonstration
examples.description: Script showing various internal and external commands and how to identify them
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Internal commands (builtins)
echo "=== Internal Commands ==="
type cd
type pwd
echo "Current directory: $(pwd)"

# External commands
echo -e "\n=== External Commands ==="
type ls
type grep
which ls

# Checking command type
for cmd in cd ls echo grep; do
 echo -n "$cmd is: "
 type -t $cmd
done
examples.explanation: Demonstrates the difference between internal and external commands using the 'type' command which shows whether a command is builtin, file, alias, or function.

examples.title: Command Combination Examples
examples.description: Script demonstrating different ways to combine commands
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Semicolon - always executes
echo "=== Semicolon (;) ==="
cd /tmp; pwd; echo "All executed"

# AND operator - executes only if previous succeeds
cd /nonexistent 2>/dev/null && echo "cd succeeded" || echo "cd failed"

# OR operator - executes only if previous fails
cd /tmp || echo "cd failed"

# Pipe - connect output to input
echo -e "line1\nline2\nline3" | wc -l

# Command substitution
TODAY=$(date +%Y-%m-%d)
echo "Today is: $TODAY"
FILES=$(ls | wc -l)
echo "Number of files: $FILES"
examples.explanation: Shows practical examples of semicolon (;), &&, ||, pipe (|), and command substitution operators in shell scripting.

examples.title: Exit Status and Conditional Execution
examples.description: Script demonstrating exit status checking and conditional execution
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Checking exit status using $?
ls /root
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
 echo "ls succeeded (exit code: $?)"
else
 echo "ls failed (exit code: $?)"
fi

# Using && and || for conditional execution
mkdir -p backup && echo "Directory created" || echo "Failed to create"

# Combining with test command
if [ -f "/etc/passwd" ] && [ -r "/etc/passwd" ]; then
 echo "passwd exists and is readable"
fi

# Exit status in function
success_func() { return 0; }
fail_func() { return 1; }

success_func && echo "Success function returned 0"
fail_func || echo "Fail function returned non-zero"
examples.explanation: Demonstrates how to check and use exit status ($?) for conditional execution using if statements and && || operators.

examples.title: Subshell vs Current Shell
examples.description: Demonstrating the difference between subshell and current shell execution
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Subshell execution with ()
OUTER_VAR="original"
(echo "Inside subshell, VAR=$OUTER_VAR")
(OUTER_VAR="modified in subshell")
echo "After subshell, VAR=$OUTER_VAR" # Still original

# Current shell execution with {}
echo -e "\n=== Curly Braces ==="
{ OUTER_VAR="modified"; echo "Inside braces, VAR=$OUTER_VAR"; }
echo "After braces, VAR=$OUTER_VAR" # Modified

# Using source
echo -e "\n=== Source Command ==="
echo "VAR=$OUTER_VAR" > /tmp/test_source.sh
echo "VAR=from_source" >> /tmp/test_source.sh
source /tmp/test_source.sh
echo "After source, VAR=$OUTER_VAR" # Changed to from_source
examples.explanation: Shows how parentheses create a subshell (changes don't persist) while curly braces execute in current shell. Also demonstrates source command.

examples.title: Pipeline Commands
examples.description: Complex pipeline examples demonstrating data processing
examples.code:
#!/bin/bash
# Count files by extension
ls -1 | grep '\.' | sed 's/.*\.//' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn

# Find largest files
du -h /tmp 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -5

# Process list filtered and sorted
ps aux | grep bash | grep -v grep | awk '{print $2, $11}' | sort

# Word frequency in file
cat /tmp/sample.txt 2>/dev/null | tr -cs '[:alnum:]' '\n' | tr '[:upper:]' '[:lower:]' | sort | uniq -c | sort -rn | head -10

# Combined pipeline with error handling
cat /etc/passwd 2>/dev/null | grep root | cut -d: -f1,7 | while read line; do
 echo "User info: $line"
done
examples.explanation: Demonstrates practical pipelines for data processing including filtering, sorting, counting, and transforming data using multiple commands connected by pipes.
